Abstract
--------

Recent advancements in Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) aim to bridge the gap between complex artificial intelligence (AI) models and human understanding, fostering trust and usability in AI systems. However, challenges persist in comprehensively interpreting these models, hindering their widespread adoption. This study addresses these challenges by exploring recently emerging techniques in XAI. The primary problem addressed is the lack of transparency and interpretability in AI models to humanity for institution-wide use, which undermines user trust and inhibits their integration into critical decision-making processes. Through an in-depth review, this study identifies the objectives of enhancing the interpretability of AI models and improving human understanding of their decision-making processes. Various methodological approaches, including post-hoc explanations, model transparency methods, and interactive visualization techniques, are investigated to elucidate AI model behaviours. We further present techniques and methods to make AI models more interpretable and understandable to humans including their strengths and weaknesses to demonstrate promising advancements in model interpretability, facilitating better comprehension of complex AI systems by humans. In addition, we provide the application of XAI in local use cases. Challenges, solutions, and open research directions were highlighted to clarify these compelling XAI utilization challenges. The implications of this research are profound, as enhanced interpretability fosters trust in AI systems across diverse applications, from healthcare to finance. By empowering users to understand and scrutinize AI decisions, these techniques pave the way for more responsible and accountable AI deployment.

1 Introduction
--------------

Artificial Intelligence (AI) has played an increasingly significant role in using data collected from diverse sources such as social media, sensors, education, multimedia, and text data, to make necessary decisions that have helped humans [[67](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR67 "Ikegwu AC, Nweke HF, Anikwe CV, Alo UR, Okonkwo OR (2022) Big data analytics for data-driven industry: a review of data sources, tools, challenges, solutions, and research directions. Clust Comput 25(5):3343–3387"),[68](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR68 "Ikegwu AC, Nweke HF, Mkpojiogu E, Anikwe CV, Igwe SA, Alo UR (2024) Recently emerging trends in big data analytic methods for modeling and combating climate change effects. Energy Inf 7(1):6"),[69](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR69 "Jan Z, Ahamed F, Mayer W, Patel N, Grossmann G, Stumptner M, Kuusk A (2023) Artificial intelligence for industry 4.0: systematic review of applications, challenges, and opportunities. Expert Syst Appl 216:119456")]. Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) stems from the increasing integration of AI models in a way that allows humans to understand, interpret, and trust the decisions and outputs generated by AI systems because these systems have become more sophisticated and pervasive. There is a growing need for transparency and interpretability in decision-making processes. With confidence in the deployment of AI by industries and governments, users are seeking enhanced transparency facilitated by XAI systems where the author introduces XAI concepts and provides an overview of areas requiring additional investigation, such as type-2 fuzzy logic systems [[58](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR58 "Hanif A, Zhang X, Wood S (2021) A survey on explainable artificial intelligence techniques and challenges. In: 2021 IEEE 25th international enterprise distributed object computing workshop (EDOCW) (pp. 81–89). IEEE.")]. The aim guarantees these systems are comprehensible and analyzable by users without specialized expertise. Understanding how AI models arrive at specific conclusions or recommendations is important for building trust and acceptance among users, stakeholders, and society. Ensuring that AI systems are accurate and interpretable helps address concerns related to bias, fairness, and unintended consequences [[98](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR98 "Lythe M, de Cos GM, Mingallon M, Lensen A, Galloway C, Knox D, Auvaa S, Kumarasinghe K (2023) Explainable AI–building trust through understanding.")]. XAI techniques enable practitioners and regulators to assess the ethical implications of AI decisions, identify potential biases, and mitigate any adverse effects which are particularly relevant in fields such as healthcare, finance, manufacturing, and criminal justice, where the consequences of AI decisions can have significant real-world impact [[123](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR123 "Pushkarna M, Zaldivar A, Kjartansson O (2022) Data cards: purposeful and transparent dataset documentation for responsible AI. In: Proceedings of the 2022 ACM conference on fairness, accountability, and transparency (pp 1776–1826).")].

Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) lies in its potential to bridge the gap between the complexity of advanced AI models and the need for human understanding and trust. As AI systems continue to play an increasingly pivotal role in critical decision-making processes, it is imperative to enhance their interpretability, making their inner workings more transparent and accessible to users. This not only promotes accountability and ethical use of AI but also addresses concerns related to unintended concerns in empowering individuals, regulators, and stakeholders to comprehend and scrutinize AI models, fostering a responsible and trustworthy integration of artificial intelligence into various aspects of society. In an earlier study by Nott [[114](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR114 "Nwakanma CI, Ahakonye LA, Njoku JN, Odirichukwu JC, Okolie SA, Uzondu C, Ndubuisi Nweke CC, Kim D (2023) Applied sciences explainable artificial intelligence ( XAI ) for intrusion detection and mitigation in intelligent connected vehicles a review. Appl Sci 13:1252")], XAI makes AI more transparent and explainable, addressing the “black box” problem. However, the results achieved do not clearly explain how or why they arrived at those conclusions. Taghikhah et al. [[145](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR145 "Talaat FM, Aljadani A, Badawy M, Elhosseini M (2024) Toward interpretable credit scoring: integrating explainable artificial intelligence with deep learning for credit card default prediction. Neural Comput Appl 36(9):4847–4865")] and Adadi and Berrada [[2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR2 "Adadi A, Berrada M (2018) Peeking inside the black box: a survey on explainable artificial intelligence (XAI). IEEE Access 6:52138–52160")] presented "Cracking open the black box of AI" refers to the concept of making AI systems more transparent and understandable. AI often operates as a "black box" because it can be challenging to interpret how these systems arrive at their decisions or predictions, especially in complex deep learning models and other inner workings of AI systems. Cracking opens the black box of AI, which is essential for building trust in AI systems, ensuring that AI operates ethically, and allowing users to understand and verify AI decisions. Nonetheless, these require much critical attention and development as AI continues to be integrated into various aspects of society [[155](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR155 "Voulodimos A, Doulamis N, Doulamis A, Protopapadakis E (2018). Deep learning for computer vision: A brief review. Computational intelligence and neuroscience, 2018.")].

To make AI processes more understandable, several key strategies in XAI techniques like model-agnostic interpretability methods can be employed and incorporated to enable users to interpret and comprehend the decisions made by complex AI models [[37](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR37 "Dwivedi R, Dave D, Naik H, Singhal S, Omer R, Patel P, Ranjan R (2023) Explainable AI (XAI): core ideas, techniques, and solutions. ACM Comput Surv 55(9):1–33")]. Also, simpler and more interpretable models whenever possible are used instead of highly complex black-box models for transparency to provide clear and concise explanations for AI outputs, including the factors influencing decisions, and enhance user understanding. Furthermore, fostering transparency in the data used to train these models, addressing biases, and regularly auditing and validating AI systems contribute to the overall interpretability and trustworthiness of AI processes because collaborative efforts between AI developers, domain experts, and end-users in the design and deployment stages also play a crucial role in making AI processes more understandable and aligned with human values and expectations [[37](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR37 "Dwivedi R, Dave D, Naik H, Singhal S, Omer R, Patel P, Ranjan R (2023) Explainable AI (XAI): core ideas, techniques, and solutions. ACM Comput Surv 55(9):1–33")]. Application of XAI in diverse areas such as healthcare, finance, legal, autonomous vehicles, robotics, etc. enhances the interpretability and understanding of AI models for humans. For instance, Amann et al. [[5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR5 "Amann J, Blasimme A, Vayena E, Frey D, Madai VI,Precise4Q Consortium (2020) Explainability for artificial intelligence in healthcare: a multidisciplinary perspective. BMC Med Inf Decis Mak, 20, pp 1–9")] utilized XAI to assist clinicians to comprehend and trust complex medical diagnoses and treatment recommendations made by AI systems, ensuring that decisions align with medical expertise. Rane et al. [[127](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR127 "Rasheed MH, Khalid J, Ali A, Rasheed MS, Ali K (2024) Human resource analytics in the era of artificial intelligence: leveraging knowledge towards organizational success in Pakistan. J Chin Hum Resour Manag 15(3):3–20. 
                  https://doi.org/10.47297/wspchrmWSP2040-800501.20241503
                  
                ")] implored XAI in finance to help in explaining algorithmic trading strategies and risk assessment, enabling financial professionals and regulators to make informed decisions and maintain market transparency. In legal and criminal justice systems, XAI assists in explaining the rationale behind AI-generated predictions, ensuring fairness and accountability [[90](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR90 "Linheiro ES, Shinde GR, Mahalle PN, Mirajkar R (2023) Explainable AI (XAI) for agriculture. Industry 4.0 convergence with AI, IoT, big data and cloud computing: fundamentals, challenges and applications, 161.")]. More so, Fernández Llorca, and Gómez [[46](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR46 "Fresz B, Dubovitskaya E, Brajovic D, Huber MF, Horz C (2024) How should AI decisions be explained? Requirements for explanations from the perspective of European Law. In: Proceedings of the AAAI/ACM Conference on AI, Ethics, and Society (Vol. 7, pp. 438–450).")] utilized XAI in autonomous vehicles and robotics, to enhance the transparency of decision-making processes, fostering user trust and safety. These applications underscore the importance of XAI in promoting transparency, accountability, and user comprehension across various domains where AI plays a pivotal role.

Despite the promising potential, Saeed and Omlin [[136](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR136 "Samek W, Wiegand T, Müller KR (2017) Explainable artificial intelligence: understanding, visualizing, and interpreting deep learning models. arXiv preprint 
                  arXiv:1708.08296
                  
                .")] reported that XAI faces several challenges in enhancing the interpretability and understanding of AI models for humans, such as the inherent trade-off between model complexity and interpretability, as highly sophisticated models often sacrifice transparency for performance. Also, developing commonly accepted standards for interpretability poses a significant challenge, as different domains may require tailored explanations, and resolving the issue of context-aware explanations is another obstacle, as the interpretability of AI models should account for the specific needs and knowledge levels of diverse user groups. Striking a balance between accuracy and simplicity in explanation generation remains a persistent challenge, especially in complex, high-dimensional datasets, Kaushik [[75](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR75 "Kesari A, Sele D, Ash E, Bechtold S (2024) A Legal framework for explainable artificial intelligence. Center for Law & Economics Working Paper Series, 9.")]. Furthermore, Chamola, et al. [[25](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR25 "Chamola V, Hassija V, Sulthana AR, Ghosh D, Dhingra D, Sikdar B (2023) A review of trustworthy and explainable artificial intelligence (xai). IEEE Access.")] reviewed that ensuring that XAI methods effectively handle real-world scenarios, including unforeseen situations and adversarial attacks, is essential for their practical applicability, and overcoming these challenges is imperative for the widespread adoption of XAI, ensuring that AI systems are not only accurate but also transparent, trustworthy, and accessible to a broad range of users.

Progressively, various studies have attempted to address these peculiar challenges. For instance, Lythe et al. [[99](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR99 "Mahbooba B, Timilsina M, Sahal R, Serrano M (2021) Explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) to enhance trust management in intrusion detection systems using decision tree model. Complexity 2021:1–11")] pinpointed that interpretability goes beyond explainability, aiming to make AI models more accessible to individuals, particularly domain experts unfamiliar with intricate AI techniques, by demystifying complex model architectures. Felzmann et al. [[45](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR45 "Fernández Llorca D, Gómez E (2021) Trustworthy autonomous vehicles. Publications Office of the European Union, Luxembourg, p 30942")] discussed that transparency in AI involves disclosing information about data, algorithms, and processes, enhancing visibility throughout the AI development and deployment stages. Additionally, ongoing research focuses on developing various explainable AI techniques, such as feature attribution methods, rule-based models, and attention mechanisms that unveil influential aspects of input data in model decisions. However, to our knowledge, none of these studies provides a comprehensive approach in solving the inherent issues beclouding the deployment of AI. Ehsan et al. [[39](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR39 "El Makhloufi A (2023) AI application in transport and logistics: opportunities and challenges (An Exploratory Study).")] qualitatively explain how an individual's AI background can shape their interpretations, highlighting differences through the perspectives of appropriation and cognitive heuristics. The study finds that both groups exhibited undue trust in numerical data for different reasons and appreciated various explanations beyond their intended design. These findings, which have significant implications for XAI, reveal that AI-generated explanations can inadvertently lead to negative consequences and potentially harmful manipulation of trust. The authors propose design interventions to address these issues.

Conversely, this paper addresses a significant research gap in the field of artificial intelligence (AI) because it primarily revolves around the pressing need for AI models to be more transparent and interpretable to humans. Therefore, the contributions of this paper to the current body of knowledge are as follows:

*   We provided a comprehensive understanding of the recent emerging techniques in XAI that address an in-depth dimension of explanatory;

*   We investigated and evaluated the efficacy of specific techniques in XAI, such as sensitivity analysis, rule-based models, documentation and transparency methods, prototype-based explanations, and visualization techniques;

*   Provides the strengths, weaknesses, and features of AI components, including machine learning, natural language processing, computer vision, knowledge representation and reasoning, robotics, and expert systems;

*   We explore an in-depth utilization of XAI techniques in diverse local use cases spanning healthcare diagnostics, financial risk assessment, retail and e-commerce, agriculture, education, transport, and logistics;

*   We further identified and analyzed the challenges associated with deploying XAI techniques in local use cases across different domains;

The rest of the paper is organized into the following sections: Sect.[2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#Sec2) delves into the explanatory depth of XAI. Section[2.1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#Sec3) outlines techniques and methods to enhance the interpretability and understandability of AI models for humans, while Sect.[2.2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#Sec4) discusses the understandability of AI components in detail. Furthermore, Sect.[3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#Sec5) gave an in-depth discussion on the deployment of AI explainable in local use cases while Sect.[4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#Sec6) covered challenges and solutions of AI explainable in local use cases. Finally, Sect.[5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#Sec7) handled areas on open research direction.

2 Explanatory Depth of Explainable AI
-------------------------------------

In the context of XAI, explanatory depth refers to the extent of detail and clarity in the explanations offered by AI systems. Some works have provided explanatory depth on XAI; Chromik et al. [[30](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR30 "Chromik M, Eiband M, Buchner F, Krüger A, Butz A (2021) I think I get your point, AI! The illusion of explanatory depth in explainable AI. In: 26th International Conference on Intelligent User Interfaces (pp. 307–317).")] stated that explanatory depth shows how deeply an AI system can explain its decisions, going beyond seeming or shallow explanations and the concept of explanatory depth is vital because not all AI explanations are equally informative or useful. Some AI systems may provide high-level, imprecise explanations that do not help users understand the underlying reasoning, while others can offer detailed, insightful explanations that enhance user’s trust and comprehension [[61](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR61 "Heng YS, Subramanian P (2022) A systematic review of machine learning and explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) in credit risk modelling. In: Proceedings of the future technologies conference (pp 596–614). Cham: Springer International Publishing.")]. So, Hulsen [[65](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR65 "Ibrahim R, Shafiq MO (2023) Explainable convolutional neural networks: a taxonomy, review, and future directions. ACM Comput Surv 55(10):1–37")] in his work stated that XAI gives the richness and detail of the explanations provided by AI systems in making AI more understandable and trustworthy, and achieving it involves various techniques and considerations to ensure that the explanations are meaningful and helpful to users. There are various methods used to achieve explanatory depth in XAI and these techniques include, Antoniadi et al. [[8](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR8 "Antoniadi AM, Du Y, Guendouz Y, Wei L, Mazo C, Becker BA, Mooney C (2021) Current challenges and future opportunities for XAI in machine learning-based clinical decision support systems: a systematic review. Appl Sci 11(11):5088")] reviewed that feature importance that is the most influential feature in the decision-making process is pointed out, Eberhard, [[38](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR38 "Ehsan U, Passi S, Liao QV, Chan L, Lee IH, Muller M, Riedl MO (2024) The who in XAI: how AI background shapes perceptions of AI explanations. In: Proceedings of the CHI conference on human factors in computing systems (pp 1–32).")] extracted that visualization using visual aids shows the decision process and feature interactions, and Mittelstadt et al. [[106](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR106 "Mohammed AA, Ambak K, Mosa AM, Syamsunur D (2019) Expert system in engineering transportation: a review. J Eng Sci Technol 14(1):229–252")] explained that counterfactual explanations by showing what changes in input data would have resulted in a different decision and local verse global explanations that offer insight into how a specific decision was made (local) as well as how the overall model behaves (global). A recent study by Del Ser et al. [[34](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR34 "Del Ser J, Barredo-Arrieta A, Díaz-Rodríguez N, Herrera F, Saranti A, Holzinger A (2024) On generating trustworthy counterfactual explanations. Inf Sci 655:119898")], introduced a framework for generating counterfactual explanations, formulating the task as a multi-objective optimization problem that balances three goals: plausibility, change intensity, and adversarial effectiveness which uses generative adversarial networks (GANs) to model input distribution, alongside a solver for multi-objective counterfactual discovery, the framework addresses the envisaged peculiar problems. The study demonstrates across six classification tasks using image and 3D data confirm evidence of trade-offs between objectives and consistency of the generated explanations with human understanding, and the framework's ability to reveal concept-based biases and misrepresented attributes within the model's input domain. Therefore, XAI techniques, including feature importance, visualization, and counterfactual explanations, enhance explanatory depth by enabling users to comprehend and trust the decisions made by AI systems.

Post-hoc explanation is an external method used after a model is trained to clarify its predictions. For instance, Retzlaff et al. [[132](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11063-025-11732-2#ref-CR132 "Ryo M (2022) Explainable artificial intelligence and interpretable machine learning for agricultural data analysis. Artif Intell Agric 6:257–265")] introduced a decision tree that helps navigate key trade-offs in various XAI methods, guiding users in selecting from six popular XAI tools. Post-hoc methods are beneficial for explaining complex models that are not naturally interpretable, such as deep neural networks or ensemble models. Abstract
--------

Explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) is gaining importance in physiological research, where artificial intelligence is now used as an analytical and predictive tool for many medical research questions. The primary goal of XAI is to make AI models understandable for human decision-makers. This can be achieved in particular through providing inherently interpretable AI methods or by making opaque models and their outputs transparent using post hoc explanations. This review introduces XAI core topics and provides a selective overview of current XAI methods in physiology. It further illustrates solved and discusses open challenges in XAI research using existing practical examples from the medical field. The article gives an outlook on two possible future prospects: (1) using XAI methods to provide trustworthy AI for integrative physiological research and (2) integrating physiological expertise about human explanation into XAI method development for useful and beneficial human-AI partnerships.

**Keywords:** Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI), Physiology, Explainability, Interpretability, Survey

Introduction
------------

Physiology is a diverse field of research within human medicine and beyond. According to Lemoine and Pradeu [[57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR57)], physiology is an integrative, explanatory science dedicated to overarching phenomena in living beings that are seen as normal (healthy) or pathological (indicative of disease). The British physiologist Noble once pointed out that “physiological analysis requires an understanding of the functional interactions between the key components of cells, organs, and systems, as well as how these interactions change in disease states” [[86](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR86)]. This means physiology is taking a holistic view on living organisms.

Although physiology is a holistic science, it seems that physiological research has not yet exploited the full potential of new technological trends, such as explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) [[59](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR59)], to integrate existing knowledge with new data-intensive analytics. This potential could be utilized in the many research fields that apply physiological methods. This includes research on cardiovascular diseases [[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR33), [49](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR49), [84](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR84)], immunology and homeostasis [[102](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR102)], neural physiology, e.g., for research on Alzheimer’s disease [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR22)] and depression [[104](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR104)], physiology of aging [[140](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR140)] and reproduction [[117](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR117)], cell biology [[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR34)] as well as the physiology of nutrition, e.g., in research on human adipogenesis [[85](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR85)], diabetes [[141](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR141)], and effects of diet on cardiovascular diseases [[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR33)]. Last but not least, physiology plays a crucial role in tumor detection and diagnosis, e.g., based on histopathological findings using either traditional methods (e.g., TMN-based classification) or modern methods (e.g., immunoscore) [[57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR57)].

Physiology therefore offers a wide range of applications for artificial intelligence (AI), a set of methods and algorithms including techniques that learn models from data (machine learning), perform automated planning, or provide knowledge representation for automated processing [[79](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR79), [127](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR127)]. Most of the AI algorithms currently in use belong to machine learning methods. These can be used for example for regression tasks, classification, or clustering [[79](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR79), [127](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR127)]. Their approach is predictive rather than descriptive or functional.

AI is already being used in various areas of physiological research, particularly in oncology, e.g., for the detection and classification of breast cancer [[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR27)] and colorectal cancer [[95](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR95)]. AI is also applied for the analysis of cardiovascular conditions, e.g., the detection of systolic dysfunction [[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR29)] or in neurophysiology, for tasks like the detection of epileptic seizures [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR13)] and stroke prediction [[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR21)].

The vast majority of current AI approaches use so-called deep neural networks (DNN), which offer high-performance architectures but remain opaque to humans in their decisions due to their complexity [[87](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR87), [126](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR126), [131](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR131)].

Explainable artificial intelligence (XAI), as a field of research and at the same time a term for methods that are either inherently interpretable or represent an interpretable extension of opaque methods, aims to counteract this intransparency [[131](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR131)]. XAI is particularly important for the integration of humans in decision-making processes [[87](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR87)]. XAI methods create understanding in recipients of explanations about the behavior and outcomes produced by AI models, e.g., why a certain cancer type was classified in a given microscopy image [[87](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR87), [126](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR126), [130](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR130)]. Thus, XAI methods serve experts for the validation of AI models, in particular, to examine whether these models have produced results for valid reasons.

AI models depend on input data and may therefore be prone to noise, errors, or incompleteness in data [[87](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR87)]. In some medical areas, such problems can be amplified by small data sets that cannot be representative of large populations, for example, in histopathology, where false tissue annotations in whole slide images, along with very diverse tissue constellations, can lead to limited generalization in AI models [[87](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR87)]. At the same time, XAI methods can help novices to understand the domain of interest with the help of an AI model, given that it was evaluated as representative by experts.

In physiology and its related medical tasks, human-centered XAI can help to integrate human knowledge into AI models as well as to extract human-understandable concepts from AI models [[80](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR80), [87](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR87), [106](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR106)]. Established rules (such as TMN cancer classification rules [[72](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR72)]) can be integrated in the process of learning AI models to provide human guidance [[126](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR126), [130](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR130)]. Analyzing internal representations of AI models and providing human-understandable labels for them can serve as a first approximation of human knowledge [[87](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR87), [106](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR106)]. All in all, putting the human into an explanatory dialogue with AI could lead to improved collaboration and better medical decision outcomes [[56](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR56), [87](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR87)].

This review article aims for providing an overview on common XAI topics and methods. It therefore contributes a collection of 85 publications that have been retrieved from a systematic review of 200 papers and articles from the fields of medicine and artificial intelligence with a special focus on physiology. It further illustrates solved and discusses open challenges in XAI research using existing practical examples from the medical field. Based on the extensive review, the article provides a short analysis of the current state of XAI usage in physiology and gives an outlook on two possible future prospects: (1) using XAI methods to provide trustworthy AI for integrative physiological research and (2) integrating physiological expertise about human explanation into XAI method development for useful and beneficial human-AI partnerships. Ultimately, this review article’s goal is to show how XAI could help to meet the integrative demands of physiological research despite the use of data-intensive (opaque) artificial intelligence methods. To the best of my knowledge, this is the first review article that focuses on XAI for physiology and which discusses the potential of XAI to realize integrative physiology.

The review is structured as follows: first, the research area of XAI is introduced with its specific terminology in Section [2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#Sec2). Then, the methodology behind the review of recent publications that utilize XAI in the field of physiology is briefly explained in Section [3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#Sec3). Subsequently, the results of the research are presented and discussed in Section [4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#Sec4). To illustrate common XAI methods and for the discussion of open challenges, examples of XAI methods are shown afterwards, in particular, for medical applications in physiology (see Section [5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#Sec5)). The discussion and conclusion Sections ([6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#Sec6) and [7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#Sec7)) explore and summarize how physiology and XAI as research areas could mutually benefit from each other in the future.

Explainable artificial intelligence (XAI)
-----------------------------------------

In general, explanatory methods help to create understanding in recipients of explanations (the explainees) about the subject or object that is to be explained (the explanandum) with the help of phenomena or concepts the explanation is based on (the explanans) [[57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR57), [126](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR126), [131](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR131)]. In the XAI literature, the component that provides explanations for opaque AI models is often called an XAI method or explainer [[131](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR131)].

According to Carvalho et al. [[92](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR92)] and Schwalbe and Finzel [[131](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR131)], the term explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) has been first used by van Lent et al. in 2004 [[59](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR59)]. The term refers to AI that is capable of explaining itself or that is enhanced by methods that make it understandable to humans. Thus, XAI is the area of research concerned with explaining an AI system’s decision [[131](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR131)]. An idea of this kind has already been coined in 1958 when computer scientist McCarthy proposed inherently transparent AI systems using a formal language and problem-solving algorithms [[71](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR71), [125](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR125)].

Early transparent methods led to expert systems research in the 1970s and 1980s until the late 1990s [[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR19)], which later lost popularity due to integration challenges. Meanwhile, artificial neural networks became prominent, with efforts to make their decisions transparent beginning in the mid-1990s [[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR31)].

The introduction of the GDPR in 2018 emphasized the need for transparency in AI, leading to new frameworks that prioritize accountability and the “right of explanation” [[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR14), [91](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR91)]. XAI gained further attention after Gunning and Aha’s publication on explainable AI in 2019, which outlined two main goals: creating interpretable models without sacrificing performance and adopting a user-centric approach to enhance human understanding and trust in AI [[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR32)].

Significant contributions to the user-centric perspective include Miller’s 2019 paper on social science aspects of AI [[78](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR78)] and Rudin’s advocacy for interpretable models [[123](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR123)]. The XAI community now also focuses on evaluating the quality of explanations, recognizing the importance of formalized assessment metrics [[75](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR75), [122](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR122)]. Most recent works provide methods that try to map internal representations in AI models to human-understandable concepts [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR10), [106](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR106), [119](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR119)] and create explanatory dialogues to allow for multi-faceted interaction and explanation processes [[56](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR56), [87](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR87)].

A specific terminology has been established in the XAI community for the categorization of XAI methods [[75](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR75), [131](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR131)]. It is briefly introduced in the following list of terms. Note that this review does not aim for providing a comprehensive survey of existing XAI methods. Instead, its goal is to point to current XAI usage in physiological research and to open challenges with respect to integrating explanations with human-centered AI in medical fields, such as physiology.

For a more detailed overview, the interested reader may consider, for example, the recent survey paper by Schwalbe and Finzel (2023) that introduces an XAI taxonomy and presents a collection of over 50 XAI methods [[131](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR131)] and the survey article by Ali et al. [[75](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR75)] that complements XAI topics and methods with their formalization. The following list is an excerpt of terminology adapted from Schwalbe and Finzel [[131](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR131)].

*   **Interpretability:** Involves the recognition of constituents of an explanandum and the assignment of meaning to such elements.

*   **Explainability:** Refers to the creation of understanding about an explanandum.

*   **Model-agnostic explanations:** An explainer produces model-agnostic explanations if it is applicable to any type of model (any type of explanandum).

*   **Model-specific explanations:** An explainer produces model-specific explanations if it is applicable to only a certain type of model (a certain type of explanandum). The reason is that such an explainer uses internal model parameters or internal representations to produce explanations.

*   **Global explanations:** Provide insights into the reasons for an overall behavior of a model. The explanandum is the model itself. The explanans may be parts of the model or some approximation of the whole model.

*   **Local explanations:** Provide insights into the reasons for an outcome of applying a model to input data. The explanandum is the outcome of a model’s application to an individual instance. The explanans may be properties of the input instance (its features) and their influence on the model’s output.

*   **Model induction:** This describes the approach of generating a surrogate model based on inputs, constituents, or outputs of another model in order to approximate the explanandum. This term was specifically coined by Gunning and Aha [[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR32)].

*   **Deep explanation:** This describes the approach of generating explanations based on constituents or outputs of a model, the explanandum, that is usually a very complex and opaque model such as DNNs. This term was coined by Gunning and Aha [[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR32)].

*   **Interpretable models:** Such models are constructed from interpretable parts and thus usually inherently transparent (see, for example, [[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR32), [123](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR123)]).

*   **Ante hoc explanations:** Explanations are produced during model creation. This applies to interpretable AI models. The model itself can be used to generate global or local explanations.

*   **Post hoc explanations:** Explanations are produced after model creation. This applies to opaque AI models which are made transparent with the help of an external explainer. The explainer is used to generate global or local explanations either by accessing internal representations of the model (deep explanation) or by approximating the model’s behavior (surrogate model).

*   **Multimodal explanations:** Such explanations are not expressed via a singular modality, e.g., text. Instead, different representations are used (e.g., text in combination with images).

*   **Human-centered explanations:** Such explanations address the varying information needs and individual characteristics of human explainees.

This list of terms is not complete as there exists more nuanced terminology for characterizing specialized explainability techniques as well as synonyms that can not be covered altogether in this article. For example, _data explainability_ [[75](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR75)] is mentioned as another aspect in the literature to describe methods that enable reasoning on data (e.g., knowledge graphs) or allow the data’s direct characterization (e.g., summarization methods). The term _ante hoc explanations_ used in this article is also called _model explainability_ (see Ali et al. [[75](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR75)]) in other works. As this article does not discuss explanation evaluation methods, the interested reader may find a comprehensive overview in Schwalbe and Finzel [[131](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR131)] and a concise summary of explanation assessment methods in Ali et al. [[75](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#CR75)].

In the following sections, the recent works applying XAI to physiological use cases will be collected, partially illustrated, and discussed with respect to their integrative nature. First, the methodology behind the search is shortly introduced.

Review methodology
------------------

For this review paper, relevant journals (with impact factor 2.0 or higher) and conference proceedings from the fields of artificial intelligence and medicine with at least an h-index of 10 were searched with the keywords “explainable artificial intelligence, XAI, explainability, interpretability, physiology.” Common platforms (Google Scholar and PubMed) were considered for paper retrieval. Only papers published during the years 2020 to 2024 were included (first considered day, 1.1.2020; last considered day, 1.11.2024). As a further criterion, publications had to have been cited at least _5 times_ in the last three years and publications from the past five years at least _10 times_ (considering only the year of publication). Preprints and workshop papers were excluded from search results. Papers that referenced applications of XAI in physiology, but did not provide any research outcomes in this area (a survey, method or experimental results), were excluded as well. From the list of results found by PubMed and Google Scholar, only the first 100 entries (sorted by relevance in Google Scholar) have been reviewed. The keywords have been searched with a query using operators. The query was formulated as “(explainable artificial intelligence OR XAI OR explainability OR interpretability) AND physiology” to allow for synonyms and related XAI terms being equally important and to emphasize on physiology.

Review results for XAI in physiology
------------------------------------

All retrieved and included papers and articles have either a strong focus on XAI with mentions of physiology or a strong focus on physiology with applications or substantial discussions of XAI.

Querying the data bases with the aforementioned query generated a list of roughly 24,700 results as denoted by Google Scholar and a list of 51,591 results in PubMed.

From the 100 first entries returned by Google Scholar, 60 met the criteria for the minimum number of citations and for the impact factor of the respective journal or the h-index of the conference. From the 100 first entries returned by PubMed, 53 publications met the citation criterion as well as the minimum impact factor or h-index for journals or conferences. The content was reviewed for its depth in XAI focus or physiology. This left 85 articles, of which 18 were survey papers and 67 presented at least one XAI method.

All selected publications are organized in Table [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#Tab1). It sorts the alphabetically ordered articles according to (1) their article type being a survey or technical contribution using an XAI method, (2) the XAI method(s) they present[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#Fn1), (3) the journal or conference they were published in[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11958383/#Fn2), and (4) the content focus in medicine (either on physiology or more general with considerable mentions of physiology as application).

> Abstract:In recent years, Artificial Intelligence technology has excelled in various applications across all domains and fields. However, the various algorithms in neural networks make it difficult to understand the reasons behind decisions. For this reason, trustworthy AI techniques have started gaining popularity. The concept of trustworthiness is cross-disciplinary; it must meet societal standards and principles, and technology is used to fulfill these requirements. In this paper, we first surveyed developments from various countries and regions on the ethical elements that make AI algorithms trustworthy; and then focused our survey on the state of the art research into the interpretability of AI. We have conducted an intensive survey on technologies and techniques used in making AI explainable. Finally, we identified new trends in achieving explainable AI. In particular, we elaborate on the strong link between the explainability of AI and the meta-reasoning of autonomous systems. The concept of meta-reasoning is 'reason the reasoning', which coincides with the intention and goal of explainable Al. The integration of the approaches could pave the way for future interpretable AI systems.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent advancements in XAI include interactive visualizations and post-hoc explanations, enhancing model interpretability and fostering user trust in AI systems.
XAI is increasingly integrated with advanced AI models, ensuring transparency and interpretability, which is crucial for effective human-AI collaboration across diverse applications.
Future XAI research emphasizes user-centric explanations, tailoring insights to individual user needs, thereby enhancing understanding and promoting trust in AI decision-making processes.
Cross-sectoral collaboration among AI developers, ethicists, and domain experts is vital for addressing challenges in XAI, ensuring ethical deployment and effective integration of AI technologies.
The future of XAI prioritizes ethical considerations, promoting fairness and accountability in AI systems, and ensuring that AI decisions are transparent and justifiable.
